author: @ShilohSedlak
text: If you only drill holes in the square tubing and put all the pipes running straight from the top square tubing to the bottom it is basically a total waste of time.. As the gas will only travel straight from the top to the bottom.. 30"? But if you instead connect each pipe in series by either putting a U pipe connecting each end or by cutting a slit into the top of the square tubing and sliding in a airtight plate between every other pipe top and bottom to make the gas flow up and down to the end of it this would make the gas go say 30" times how ever many pipes you have.. The way you are making it will not be any different than just having one 30" pipe.. It will basically not work as a cooler at all. This is why all radiators have macaronis at each end to make the contents flow back and forth over as many feet of pipe as possible.

author: @martynjones973
text: Hi Robert just watched some Gasifier ideas on you tube, may be worth looking at blow back safety valves 👍👍👍

author: @CornishMiner
text: If you put some stainless steel pot scourers inside the cooling pipes it would greatly increase the surface area for cooling without creating too much flow resistance (works very well in reflux columns). You might also want to consider a method for draining any condensates accumulating in the cooling radiator. Great project Robert.

author: @deborahward5097
text: in my gasifier i built a turbo unit with something similar to a spinning chimney  sweep brush inside and water injection at the intake, i placed it after the cyclone filter (which did nothing ) and got very good results in both cleaning and cooling, and it also helped move the gas along the system.

replies.author: @kennyclement2823

replies.author: @Palerider1942
replies.text: @kennyclement2823 doesn't even need to be dry, one of the layers in the fire tube is using the heat produced to dry the fuel prior to the gasification process.  Now you cant have standing water in there but the thing was designed for emergency use where dry fuel may not be available

author: @Palerider1942
text: When i built my gasifier years ago (school science project) i found that there was a lot of liquid inside the radiator after a while, looked like some kind of thin creosote to me. i done mine using an old radiator from the house, lying on its side underneath the whole unit, and i didnt have a good way of removing this liquid while it was running.  if you are doing yours upright it might be worth angling your outlet square tube away from where the takeoff is going to be and include a transparent trap with some sort of bleed valve at the bottom (part of the science project was include things to improve for MK2 so i have this down) taht way the buildup of the liquid acts as a gas valve so you wont lose any product while you bleed it. then again, that might all be becuase my primary and secondary filters were not good enough. but hey, just something for you to think about

replies.author: @kennyclement2823
replies.text: A glass jar at the base,brazed in of course, dose well as a liquid trap

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's a nice suggestion mate - cheers

author: @NorthernKitty
text: This is really shaping up to be a beautiful piece of industrial art!!  I like that you pay attention to the aesthetics.  😊😊😊  Does the sawdust need to be replaced periodically?  If so, I'm wondering if perhaps some sort of gaskets might be more convenient in the long run than silicone sealant, so you don't have to redo the seals each time you replace the sawdust.

replies.author: @johnmcfadden9336
replies.text: One could use silicon wet with soapy water shape and smooth allow to set a good bit then wet again with soap or release stuff assemble gently and shape the silicon allow to fully set

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am not sure how often the sawdust will need replacing but silicon can be use to make reusable gaskets - I might do a video on just that to show how

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: Good find mate, that should work perfectly as a filter. If you needed to filter it even more you could use some stainless Steel wool like the old SOS pads. Tear them apart and spread them out. More than likely you won't need that but is an idea incase you do.

replies.author: @marwerno
replies.text: Steel wool probably also helps in the cooling down action.
